meta.title: Generic Assignments, Strain Histories and Properties of Pure Cultures of Cyanobacteria

abstractHtml: Summary: On the basis of a comparative study of 178 strains of cyanobacteria, representative of this group of prokaryotes, revised definitions of many genera are proposed. Revisions are designed to permit the generic identification of cultures, often difficult through use of the field-based system of phycological classification. The differential characters proposed are both constant and readily determinable in cultured material. The 22 genera recognized are placed in five sections, each distinguished by a particular pattern of structure and development. Generic descriptions are accompanied by strain histories, brief accounts of strain properties, and illustrations; one or more reference strains are proposed for each genus. The collection on which this analysis was based has been deposited in the American Type Culture Collection, where strains will be listed under the generic designations proposed here.
summaryHtml:
This seminal 1979 study by Rippka and colleagues presents a comprehensive revision of cyanobacterial taxonomy based on systematic analysis of 178 pure culture strains. The authors propose redefined generic definitions for 22 genera organized into five sections, each distinguished by characteristic structural and developmental patterns. The taxonomy emphasizes culture-determinable characteristics rather than relying solely on field-based phycological classification, which had proven difficult for identifying cultured isolates. The five sections are distinguished by reproductive modes: Section I comprises unicellular organisms reproducing by binary fission or budding; Section II includes organisms with multiple fission producing baeocytes; Sections III-V encompass filamentous forms with varying developmental complexity, culminating in heterocystous nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria in Sections IV and V. The authors describe maintenance media, cultivation techniques, purity control methods, and characterization approaches for structural and physiological properties. Reference strains are designated for each genus, and the collection was deposited in the American Type Culture Collection. This work established a practical framework for culture-based cyanobacterial identification and nomenclature, addressing long-standing ambiguities in applying botanical classification systems to laboratory isolates.

Key findings

  • Five major sections of cyanobacteria were defined based on reproductive modes and cellular organization: unicellular with binary fission/budding (I), multiple fission with baeocytes (II), and three filamentous types with increasing developmental complexity (III-V)
  • Generic definitions were revised to be determinable in cultured material using simple, readily observable characteristics such as cell morphology, division planes, sheath formation, and heterocyst development, rather than ecological or field-based properties
  • Twenty-two cyanobacterial genera were formally recognized and assigned reference strains; type material was intentionally avoided due to difficulties with immutable herbarium specimens, supporting efforts to bring cyanobacteria under Bacteriological Code jurisdiction
  • Standardized culture media and cultivation conditions were established for maintaining diverse cyanobacterial strains, with specific media (BG-11, MN, ASN-III) optimized for freshwater, nitrogen-fixing, and marine organisms respectively
  • Physiological characterization methods were developed to assess photoheterotrophy, nitrogen fixation, pigmentation profiles including phycoerythrinoid pigments, and DNA base composition to complement structural taxonomy
